data.title: De-Dollarization Is Accelerating
data.description:
There is a major shift underway in the global financial order. Countries are increasingly moving away from the U.S. dollar (NYSEARCA:UUP) in favor of alternative stores of value and mediums of exchange. This is seen on several levels, but perhaps most prominently in central banks’ move toward decreasing dollars as a percentage of their reserves in favor of alternatives like gold (GLD), while BRICS nations are also openly discussing establishing a competing currency or even several competing currencies to the U.S. dollar. Meanwhile, U.S. foreign policy is only further aggravating the problem that started when the Federal Reserve embarked on a decade of aggressive money printing and artificially low interest rates.

In this article, I will discuss this trend in greater detail and what its implications are for investors, and then conclude with how I am positioning my portfolio to shelter me and even generate alpha from it.

Why Central Banks Are Accelerating De-Dollarization

As previously mentioned, central banks have been buying gold in record numbers in recent years to the tune of hundreds and even thousands of tons. This buying has been led by the likes of China, India, and other major developing economies as they seek to increase their diversification away from the dollar.

This record-setting demand has been reflected in the huge boom in gold prices in recent years, and I expect the gold bull market to continue for reasons I discussed recently here.

Meanwhile, the BRICS nations are growing by recently adding new members, including Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Egypt, and are centering much of their agenda on the potential establishment of an alternative currency that could even be backed by something like gold or oil (USO) to challenge and ultimately potentially usurp the U.S. dollar as the leading global reserve currency. At the very least, it could become a highly popular medium of exchange for international commerce.

This would likely have significant impacts on the global economy, in particular the United States, as demand for Treasuries (TLT) would likely plummet and dollars currently held overseas would flow back into the U.S., leading to substantial inflation and potentially skyrocketing long-term interest rates.

How U.S. Policy Opened the Door

This dynamic did not happen in a vacuum, as it is largely a product of unsound monetary policy for over a decade that kept interest rates artificially low for a prolonged period of time, while also establishing the fiscal foundation for today’s de-dollarization trend.

Moreover, it was accelerated further by the politicization of the U.S. dollar’s reserve status, particularly the freezing of Russia’s foreign exchange reserves, much of which were in dollars. This was significant because it revealed that countries storing wealth in dollars make themselves extremely susceptible to U.S. sanctions. As the chart below illustrates, U.S. sanctions use has increased quite substantially over the past decade.

This has only served to further drive this risk home for foreign countries, pushing the likes of China—America’s top geopolitical rival—into aggressive diversification away from the dollar.

On top of that, the U.S. tariffs that have recently been levied on major trade partners have increasingly pushed major economies to look for ways to diversify away from the U.S. Dollar, as confidence in the established global economic order in which the U.S. exports dollars and imports goods is beginning to shift.

What This Means for Investors

So what does this mean for investors? It means that investing in paper dollar-denominated assets, especially those with long durations, is increasingly risky. This is why I recently cautioned against investing in long-duration fixed income assets like bonds (AGG).

Instead, I think hard assets like gold and silver (SLV), including blue-chip dividend-paying gold miners (GDX) like Newmont (NEM) and Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), as well as real estate (VNQ) and infrastructure (UTF) with plays like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC), Enbridge (ENB), and W.P. Carey (WPC), all offer inflation protection combined with inflation-linked contracts along with underlying hard assets that tend to appreciate over the long term in inflationary environments.

Investor Takeaway

While the dollar does remain the dominant global currency—it still makes up the majority of global reserves and continues to serve as the primary medium of exchange in international trade—and U.S. capital markets remain by far the deepest and most liquid in the world, the de-dollarization trend is clear and unlikely to reverse anytime soon. That said, the de-dollarization trend appears to be gradual, rather than a sudden, dramatic shift. As such, investors can get behind this trend relatively early by investing in real assets that should hold up well under a de-dollarization scenario.

Barring some major fiscal collapse in the United States or a major war in which the U.S. military, and in particular the U.S. Navy, is decisively defeated by a geopolitical foe such as China, it is likely that the de-dollarization trend will be very gradual rather than a sudden event. That said, it does not hurt for investors to begin diversifying their portfolios now so that they can benefit from this trend rather than suffer long-term headwinds from it. There is also always the possibility that a sudden event could dramatically accelerate the trend, so it is prudent to be prepared. As a result, we are overweight precious metals, infrastructure, and real estate in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: HEICO: Great Business, Great Execution, And I'd Love To Invest, But Not At This Valuation
data.description:
I wish I could upgrade. But I can’t.

Aftermarket aircraft-parts maker HEICO (NYSE: HEI) is a terrific company. I understood that when I launched coverage on 9/9/24. I also understood it when I refreshed my analysis on 12/24/24.

But as much love and respect as I had for the company, I felt compelled to say “Sell.”

I did so based solely because of valuation.

That doesn’t mean I despise the stock. For me, a Seeking Alpha Sell rating means I expect HEI shares to underperform the market. (So in a bull market, it can rise, just not as much as the overall market.)

Dichotomies like this happen very frequently on Seeking Alpha.

Surely, you’ve seen countless instances of analysts writing favorably about companies but expressing concerns about excessive stock valuations.

Those are examples of correct analysis. Companies and stocks must be evaluated separately.

You haven’t, however, seen much of that from me. Now that I’m retired and able to choose what I will or won’t write about, I tend to focus on companies I like, and usually those whose shares I find buyable — or at least, holdable.

This doesn’t mean everything I cover is modestly valued.

Quite to the contrary, I’m bullish on many stocks that others reject based on valuation. I discussed this in an 8/8/25 Palantir (PLTR) report. And I explained a different aspect of my approach on 8/24/25, in connection with Poet Technologies (POET).

Today, I’ll explain why HEI, great as it is, does not fit my criteria for tolerating very high valuations. (That I cover it at all reflects my admiration for the company.)

Let me start with the good stuff…

A Terrifically Conceived and Well-Executed Approach to Aftermarket Aircraft Parts

This is not the kind of company one should learn about by browsing a basic description.

Aircraft aftermarket spare parts refer to those not made by the manufacturer, the OEM.

So imagine a mechanic for JetBlue (JBLU) or Delta (DAL) going to an aircraft version of AutoZone (AZO). The mechanic browses the shelves, probably comparing prices. Hopefully for HEI the mechanic grabs one of its offerings. Or, perhaps, the mechanic orders on the web site.

Are you excited yet? Neither am I.

Regulators would be apoplectic!

You see, selling aftermarket aircraft parts is nothing like aftermarket auto parts.

At best, a badly made auto part means you have to shell out for anther one. If you're not fortunate, your car may mess up in a way that causes other kinds of damage. And we can‘t rule out something going wrong that causes an accident leading to injuries or worse.

But that’s nothing compared to what can happen if a bad aircraft part is used. One part failure can and does cause horrific mass-casualty disasters.

That’s why not everyone can sell aftermarket aircraft parts. To do that, you must submit each part to the FAA (Federal Aviation Administration) for study, testing, and approval.

The goal is to get Parts Manufacturer Approval (PMA).

If the part is judged to be good (airworthy) you get PMA. Then, you can sell it.

Here’s the catch — It’s not easy to get PMA. As noted, aircraft parts are mission critical. Failure is not an option. So you need to be good at doing this.

And that’s what makes HEI special. It is good at doing it. It’s particularly good at reverse engineering OEM parts!

See my prior 9/9/24 and 12/24/24 write-ups for more on HEI’s business.

In sum, HEI is good at manufacturing in a cost-efficient manner that leads to good margins. And it’s good at finding companies to acquire to add to its stable of PMA parts. And it’s good at acquiring at reasonable prices. And it doesn’t borrow excessively to fund its purchases.

We can see the results of this in the financial statement highlights shown below.

We can also see HEI’ fundamental merits in the Seeking Alpha Profitability presentation.

The Latest Quarter, Great But…

Not that I care about Wall Street’s absurd guidance game (see here and here), but I’d be remiss if I didn’t mention that HEI’s 8/26/25 earnings release and earnings call made it a big winner here.

The prize: HEI stock gained 8.8% on 8/26/25. It fell 4.7% on 8/27/25. That's life in the guidance-game arena.

What, you may ask, were the revenue and EPS numbers?

I’ll show you. But I’ll do it below in the context of discussing valuation. After all, growth and value go hand in hand. They’re two peas in a pod, inextricably intertwined.

Seriously! No matter how many gurus try to tell you otherwise, Growth and Value are NOT independent investment factors.

Every legitimate valuation model must include one or more growth inputs.

There are many ways to do this. Some approaches are better than others. We all have our respective opinions.

But failing to do it all is not an option if any valuation exercise is to be considered legitimate. (This is why it’s important to look at Seeking Alpha Value Grades together with its other grades.)

I’m fussing about this now to introduce what will come, a showing that despite really good 8/26 earnings news, this company is not growing nearly enough to justify the stock’s valuation.

I’ll also discuss why I’m not as willing to take the sort of qualitative approach to growth I’ve often taken for other tech-oriented stocks.

HEI’s Value-Growth Mismatch

Let me start with the basics:

  • HEI’s Forward P/E (based on next year’s estimated EPS) is 69.24.
  • HEI’s Forward PEG (using the projected 3- to 5-year EPS growth rate is 4.14

Note: These and all other figures I present below are as of the time I did my analysis. All these numbers are fluid so what you may see on Seeking Alpha when you look can differ. But the variations will be immaterial and won’t change the overall analysis.

This is less information than I usually give for valuation. But you don’t need a lot now. You can see at a glance that those numbers are high. And lest there be any doubt, know that Seeking Alpha assigns HEI a Valuation Grade of “F.” So we need a lot of growth to compensate for that.

I call the 69.24 P/E and 4.14 PEG ratios to your attention the way I do now because I want you to really be thinking about them as you scan the quarterly EPS and Revenue trends I’ll now present.

Not bad! Not bad at all. But are the numbers good enough?

To help you along, here are separate tables showing the year-over-year percent changes. And remember, as you scan these, that the PE is 69.24 and the PEG is 4.14.

I wish there was a science to figuring out what valuations should match up with which percent growth rates. There isn’t. (Even the cherished PEG, which should preferably not be above 1.00, is folklore, not math.

I refer back to Craveth Read, one of the greatest but least known gurus I discussed in my 8/24/25 POET report:

[I]t is a mistake to aim at an unattainable precision. It is better to be vaguely right than exactly wrong.

Anyone who has ever tried to input an infinite growth rate into a dividend growth valuation, or to compute terminal value for a discounted cash flow model has to know (at least I hope they know) Read is spot on.

So let’s aim to be vaguely right. To help us do that, we can examine valuations and growth metrics for HEI and a lot of big-name growth companies.

You’re probably very familiar with most issues I include in the upcoming table.

You may be less so with the bottom three. These are infrastructure firms that enable much of the tech-driven growth we crave. They’re EMCOR Group (EME), Primoris (PRIM) and Quanta Services (PWR).

At the bottom, I add medians for companies in the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

I also add Return on Equity and Return on Assets comparisons. These company-quality indicators are excellent proxies for business risk. The lower the risk (the higher the returns on equity and assets), the higher the justified valuation ratios. (These fit into conventional valuation models as key drivers of the discount rates you need to input. This is where PEG fails mathematically – it ignores this.)

So now – drum roll – here are the comparisons.

Don’t sweat the details of each number. Instead, take your time and get a feel for how the numbers match up.

  • Notice how HEI’s growth rates compare to others you see.
  • Consider HEI’s returns on Equity and Asset compared to others.
  • Next, scan down the first two columns that compare valuations.
  • Don’t lock in on any individual number. Get a feel for the tone of the table as a whole.
  • Finally, ask yourself every step of the way: Aren’t HEI’s valuations too high relative to growth and quality? Whatever you think of these relationships for other companies, aren’t they more favorable compared to what you see for HEI?

I come away from this table believing that Valuations-to-Metrics is far higher for HEI tan for the others.

If you disagree, then you’ll disagree with my strictly-valuation-based Sell rating for HEI. (That would be OK. Everybody can’t agree on everything. It takes two to trade, and many varied opinions to make a market!)

Why I Don’t Take a More Qualitative Approach to Growth and Profitability

If you follow my work, you know I do this a lot – a heck of a lot.

In fact, in my 8/24/25 POET writeup, I introduced a completely non-numerical “ratio” to consider. I called it S/R.

S stands for Story.

R stands for one’s personal tolerance for very high Risk.

I applied it to POET, a pre-commercial company. One can make a case for applying it to Palantir (PLTR). I originally did that. But I’m now at “Hold” because I believe the valuation is too high even for that great story. Elon Musk and Tesla (TSLA) bulls wish you would downplay the car business and focus on the solving-automation story.

As enjoyable as it is to analyze, write about and own S/R stocks, I simply cannot put HEI in that group.

To my way of thinking…

A Bona Fide S/R Stock Involves a Company That’s Working to Change the World

Biotech companies do this. So, too, do AI companies. Semiconductor companies did this back in the 1980s-90s and most are still pushing forward, even today. Many software and cyber-security companies are working to do this.

An important aspect of a true change-the-world company is the newness of what it’s doing.

  • It has to be new to the point that analysts have no historical or other data on which they can base forecasts.
  • And it has to be new enough to follow relevant historical precedent to the effect that society often heavily underestimates how big new things can become. (I’ve seen this constantly over my life — with internet, e-commerce, wireless, streaming… so many things that are now ubiquitous.)
  • As a result, there needs to be a substantial and credible likelihood that even the more optimistic among today’s growth-rate projections will turn out to be heavily underestimated.

Some companies were clear change-the-world firms at one time but may no longer be (and may be trying to get back to that). Apple (AAPL) is one example.

Others are hybrid. Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL) and Meta (META) combine ordinary operations with world-change aspirations.

There’s no simple formula. If there is, none of us would ever own a stock that winds up generating only average or below-average performance.

But I feel quite confident in saying…

HEI is Not a Change-The-World Company

It operates in a very basic business.

That’s not necessarily a bad thing. In fact, HEI does what it does very very well. It’s arguably the best at what it does.

But it’s still functioning within the world in which we live today.

When things go well for HEI, as they are right now, it’s not happening because HEI is creating new demand for a new product or service.

It’s happening because, well… things are going well.

Spares for the newest generation of planes are bought from the manufacturers… OEM parts. Airlines are careful to avoid knocking out their warranties. So HEI’s parts, great as they may be (PMA certified!), are usually bought for older planes.

HEI’s business will strengthen or settle back depending on the usage of older planes. Right now, there’s a lot of that. But this is something that ebbs and flows over time. Supply chain bottlenecks or lacks thereof are also part of the picture.

I could go on about this. But I won’t because I fear if I do, I’d wind up distracting from the main issue here…

Ups and downs of PMA parts demand and pricing are based on normal commercial/business trends. It’s not about changing the world. It’s about good or bad times within the existing world.

Change-the-world does not lend itself to numerical analysis. Doing business within the existing world is amenable to numerical analysis.

I’d be much more interested in HEI’s quarter- or year-ahead prospects, trends in newer-versus-older aircraft usage, etc. if HEI’s forward P/E were, say 30-ish. I could then analyze more deeply to see it the stock is really worth that, versus say a low-to mid-20s P/E.

But a P/E of 60-plus dramatically changes the picture.

Absent much faster and more structurally sustainable growth than anything we’ve seen or are likely to see from HEI, I can’t get behind this stock… no matter how much respect and admiration I feel for the way the company runs its business.

I feel this way even though my two prior Sell recommendations haven’t, as of today, aged well.

  • Since my initial 9/9/24 report, HEI is up 24%, versus a 19% gain for the S&P 500.
  • Since my subsequent 12/24/24 report, HEI is up 33%, versus a 9% gain for the S&P 500.

I really tried hard to see what I may have missed that the market is seeing. I came up empty, except perhaps for lingering euphoria relating to air travel’s post-pandemic recovery-surge. So I’m sticking to my guns.

Risk

Given HEI’s current valuation-metrics mismatch, there’s a major risk every day when the stock starts trading.

There’s also guidance-game risk every time the company reports a new quarter.

And as with any persistent acquirer, there’s risk the next deal may not go as well as others have gone.

What to do About HEI Stock

The price chart gives us insight to Mr. Market’s attitude.

For much of the lead-up to the latest earnings release Mr. Market was all in.

The 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) was strong relative to the through the 50-day EMA.

Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO) also were bullish.

Both indicators measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general. And both were really hot.

As earnings date neared and thereafter, it looks as if Mr. Market may have been showing signs of a hangover. It looks like he may have drank too much champagne celebrating guidance game victories. Or perhaps he noticed it was raining (i.e., the stock was very highly valued) when he finally left the party and stepped outside.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

Based solely on valuation, I see HEI as likely underperforming the broader market.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by reluctantly continuing to rate HEI “Sell.”

data.title: AMD: Left In Nvidia's Dust As Valuations Reach Excessive Levels (Downgrade)
data.description:
AMD's Rally Has Occurred Overly Fast & Furious, With It Triggering A Mixed Return Profile

We previously covered Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) in May 2025, discussing how it remained a viable rebound investing story, thanks to the overly discounted valuations, the robust Free Cash Flow generation, and the healthy balance sheet.

Combined with the growing x86 market share, the discounted valuations and the $78s trading floor, we had believed that the semiconductor company had been well positioned to grow its client/ data center CPU and data center sales during an ongoing cloud super cycle.

AMD 1Y Stock Price

Since then, AMD has had an eye-watering rally by +50.4%, with it well outperforming the wider market at +11.2% and a similar upward momentum also observed in the wider semiconductor market since the April 2025 bottom.

Much of their tailwinds are attributed to the undeniable durability of the data center-related spending trends in the Q2 '25 earnings season, as reported by numerous market leaders across the foundry, infrastructure, SaaS, REIT, and even, energy layers.

With the AI hype already entering new heights after the worst of the tariff-induced doom and gloom in April 2025, it is unsurprising that the market has rebounded quickly to retest new peaks while awarding the beneficiaries' bright prospects with double/ triple digits recoveries thus far.

Even so, we believe that AMD's upward momentum by +115.5% since the April 2025 bottom has been overly done, attributed to the underwhelming FQ2'25 performance metrics which underscores its inability to capitalize and monetize the ongoing data center boom while still being left in the dust by the clear market leader, Nvidia (NVDA) at +90.9% over the same time period.

For reference, AMD has reported a mixed Data Center revenue performance at $3.24B (-11.7% QoQ/ +14.4% YoY), attributed to the "strong demand for AMD EPYC™ processors more than offsetting headwinds impacting AMD Instinct MI308 shipments to China," compared to NVDA's Data Center performance over a similar period at $41.09B (+5% QoQ/ +56.4% YoY).

With Q2 '25 bringing forth nearly zero China-related AI chip sales for both semiconductor companies, attributed to the ongoing US export restriction, it is immediately apparent that NVDA's AI related offerings remain the more popular choice as the Blackwell chips' revenue also grow by +17% sequentially.

While AMD has reported robust Gaming Segment revenue growth to $1.12B (+75% QoQ/ +75% YoY) against NVDA's Gaming Segment revenue growth to $4.28B (+13.8% QoQ/ +48.6% YoY), it is apparent that the Jensen Huang-led company remains the King of GPUs thus far.

The only silver lining to AMD's investment thesis remains the Client Segment, where they report robust revenue growth to $2.49B (+8.7% QoQ/ +67.1% YoY) along with the expansion of its x86 CPU revenue share to 33% in Q2'25 (+1.3 points QoQ/ +8.8 YoY) and unit share to 24.2% (-0.2 points QoQ/ +2.9 YoY).

The renewed x86 growth profile has naturally occurred at the expense of another legacy player, Intel (INTC), whom has been consistently reporting deteriorating Desktop x86 market share to 67.8% in Q2'25 and Server CPU market share to 72.7%, compared to Q4'19 levels of 81.7% and 95.5%, respectively.

This development underscores AMD's black horse position in the x86 race, with it perhaps explaining why the market has rallied behind its ongoing recovery, despite failing to fully partake in the AI and data center boom.

This is especially since the Client/ Gaming Segment has been reporting richer operating margins of 21.1% (-0.5 points QoQ/ +10 YoY/ +1.2 from FY2022 levels of 19.9%/ +8.9 from FY2019 overall computing and graphic margins of 12.2%).

These well balances the carnage observed in the Data Center Segment at operating margins of -4.7% (-30 points QoQ/ -30.9 YoY), partially attributed to a $800M in inventory/ related charges from the export controls, and the Embedded Segment at 33.3% (-6.5 points QoQ/ -6.7 YoY), respectively.

Given the mixed performance metrics in FQ2 '25, we believe that AMD's recent recovery has occurred overly fast and furious indeed, with the stock merely lifted by the exuberant market sentiments instead of fundamentals.

A similarly mixed FQ3 '25 outlook has been offered by the management as well, with revenues of $8.7B (+13.2% QoQ/ +27.7% YoY) against that of NVDA's at $54B (+15.5% QoQ/ +53.9% YoY), despite both companies yet to include any sales arising from China.

While AMD may have touted the increased adoption by supercomputers, we believe that these units are far and few in between, with it apparent that many hyperscalers remain locked in to NVDA's vertically integrated CUDA platform, networking solutions (hardware/ NVLink software), and new CPU offerings, amongst other things.

This is especially since more hyperscalers have adopted Arm-based computing platform (ARM), as observed in NVDA's recent launch of Arm-based Grace Blackwell superchip, with the former already expecting "the market share of Arm Neo-based chips to top hyperscalers to reach nearly 50% this year" while naturally presenting intensified competition to AMD's server segment.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

For now, thanks to the decent Data Center uptake and the still robust Client Segment growth, we can understand why the consensus forward estimates remain somewhat promising, with AMD expected to report a top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +22%/ +30.9% through FY2027.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +15.1%/ +27.5% in May 2025, while being not too far from the 5Y historical growth profile at +30.8%/ +38.9% and the 10Y growth at +16.7%/ +49.3%, respectively.

AMD Valuations

Even so, given its mixed growth profile, we believe that the market's ongoing upgrading of AMD's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 42.72x has been overly done indeed, compared to the prior Buy article levels of 29.26x, the 5Y mean of 39.47x, and the 10Y mean of 39.67x.

While AMD may appear to be reasonably valued at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.39x, compared to its direct computing platform/ semiconductor peers, including:

  • INTC at PEG non-GAAP ratio mean of 0.82x (prior to the PC demand destruction in 2022),
  • NVDA currently at 1.30x,
  • ARM currently at 3.37x,
  • Broadcom (AVGO) currently at 1.99x, and
  • Marvell (MRVL) currently at 0.75x,

it remains rather expensive compared to our prior Buy article levels of 1.12x, the 5Y mean of 1.22x, and the 10Y mean of 0.80x.

This development implies that a moderate pullback to our Buy zones may be necessary for an improved return profile, one that we shall further discuss in the next segment.

So, Is AMD Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

AMD 5Y Stock Price

For now, AMD has had a hyperbolic upward momentum since the April 2025 bottom, albeit unable to breakout of its 2024 resistance levels of $180s over the past few weeks, with the stock seemingly losing steam by the time of writing.

This cadence is unsurprising indeed, since the stock is trading at a notable premium compared to our base-case fair value estimates of $101.50, based on the LTM adj EPS of $3.45 (+24.1% sequentially) and the 1Y P/E mean of 29.43x.

AMD has also pulled forward much of its upside potential to our base-case long-term price target of $218.60, based on the consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $7.43, with it offering interested investors with a minimal margin of safety.

While there remains an excellent upside potential of +88.2% to our bull-case long-term price target of $317.40, assuming that the stock is able to sustain its currently elevated FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 42.72x (not too far from its 5Y mean of 39.47x), we believe that its rebound has occurred relatively fast and furious, with traders likely to soon take their gains off the table.

AMD's Reversing Momentum

This conjecture is not overly bearish as well, attributed to AMD's seemingly reversing momentum by the time of writing, with it remaining to be seen if the stock may be able to retain much of its recent gains, as the triple digits rally since the April 2025 bottom exhausts much of the investors' buying power as observed in the technical indicators above.

While the AI/ CPU market may be big enough for numerous market players, we believe that a lower dollar cost average is critical for a robust long-term return profile - one that is highly critical given the company's laggard status in the recent race, pending a product launch that changes the status quo.

Combined with the inability to break out of the $180s, the recent retracement to the $160s ranges, and the premium valuations painting an overly optimistic picture about its lackluster AI/ mixed CPU prospects, we prefer to downgrade our rating for AMD stock to a Hold here.

Interested Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. investors may consider waiting for a deep retracement to occur, preferably somewhere between our base-case fair value estimates of $100s and our bull-case fair value estimates of $147.30 (based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 42.72x and consensus FY2027 adj EPS estimates of $7.43) for an improved margin of safety, with those levels also nearer to the 100/ 200 day moving averages.

Given the -26.6% downside at the midpoint from current levels, some patience may be more prudent indeed.

data.title: Alibaba's 9% Earnings Rally: The AI Bet Is Paying Off
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding Limited (NYSE:BABA) just released its earnings for the quarter ended June 2, 2025. The release missed revenue estimates by $910 million, beat estimates on GAAP earnings, and missed on adjusted earnings by 14%. The release nevertheless triggered a 9% rally at market open, as there was a lot of good news within individual segments.

Particularly noteworthy was the performance in the cloud segment, which saw revenue accelerate from 18% year over year to 26% year over year. Heading into the release, investors were concerned about the impact of the company’s heavy spending on food delivery services. The company pledged to spend $7 billion on food delivery subsidies in 2025, which is nearly half a typical year’s profit. News of Alibaba’s food delivery subsidies appeared to cause significant volatility in the stock the week that they were announced, as the stock fell 4.6% that week. As we saw in the company’s June quarter release, strength in the cloud segment was more than enough to put those fears to rest.

Heading into Alibaba’s earnings release, investors had mixed expectations. While some were optimistic about strength in the company’s cloud segment, others worried about the effects of the food delivery price war on the company–with good reason, because the other two companies involved in the war crashed after earnings. In the end, the cloud segment’s performance won out in investors’ eyes, as indicated by the fact that the stock immediately launched into a 9% rally at the open. The price continued holding up at the time this article was submitted.

As for me personally, I was expecting good things heading into Alibaba’s release. I knew that the cloud segment’s results would be strong, because management guided for strong cloud segment results in the most recent earnings call. I figured that cloud revenue would be up more than 20% in the June quarter. That was precisely what happened, with segment revenue up 26%, and segment earnings up by the same amount.

When I last covered Alibaba stock, I rated it a Strong Buy on the grounds that it was China’s clear generative AI leader. Since then, the stock has risen in price only slightly. The company has also put out an earnings release that, while technically a miss, was very strong in terms of the most important segments’ results. Because Alibaba’s results confirmed my bullish thesis, and because the stock price is not up too much, I’m maintaining Alibaba at Strong Buy with a near term price target of $154. In the ensuing paragraphs I explain my reasoning for this take.

Earnings Recap

Before getting into the forward looking aspects of my thesis, I should explore Alibaba’s recent earnings release, because it’s the most important recent news about the company, and because some items in it line up nicely with my long term thesis.

Alibaba’s most recent earnings release was a pretty good one, boasting metrics such as:

  • Overall revenue up 2% to $34.57 billion (miss by $910 million).

  • Revenue after disposals up 10% year over year (put differently: the businesses Alibaba still owns grew 10%, while the sale of some underperforming segments hurt the 'official' amount).

  • Operating income down 3% to $4.88 billion.

  • Adjusted EBITA down 14% to $5.8 billion.

  • Reported (GAAP) net income up 76% to $5.9 billion.

  • Cash from operations down 39% to $2.88 billion.

  • Negative free cash flow of $2.6 billion.

The release technically missed estimates, and indeed, some of the numbers (e.g., the revenue figure and the negative free cash flow) looked bad. However, these are easily explained:

  • Revenue growth decelerated because Alibaba sold off two perpetual money-losing businesses, which was a wise capital allocation decision.

  • The negative free cash flow was due to large investment in data center infrastructure capital expenditure (CAPEX), An important growth category for a company that just delivered results in its most important segments.

So, despite the apparently negative results in some categories, Alibaba executed on its strategic priorities in the June quarter, and also achieved solid top-line growth on a “like on like” basis. It was a pretty good showing.

Future Prospects

Having looked at Alibaba’s most recent earnings release, we can now turn to its future prospects. It’s here that the company’s massive AI spending starts to make sense. Alibaba is spending $53 billion over a period of three years to make itself into the cloud computing and AI powerhouse of Asia. The spending is already starting to bear fruit, as seen in Alibaba Cloud’s massive June quarter growth. Also, the company recently announced new data centers in Malaysia, the Philippines and Thailand. Malaysia is an advanced high tech economy, Thailand has a burgeoning tech startup scene, and the Philippines is a frontier market with a long growth runway ahead of it. So, Alibaba has a lot of current and future potential customers in these markets.

As for the infrastructure Alibaba is building, it mainly consists of data centers running lots of AI applications. These include custom applications built by the company’s clients, Alibaba AI tools sold to customers by token, and Alibaba tools for end users, such as Qwen chat.

These are all promising growth initiatives. The end goal is ultimately to turn Alibaba Cloud into the Amazon Web Services (AWS) of Asia, the leader in cloud services in the region. With AWS doing about $108 billion in annual revenue on its own, this opportunity could change the conversation about Alibaba completely. The company does $137 billion currently, so it could virtually double its top line if it pulls off a “best case scenario” with Alibaba Cloud.

Valuation

Despite its massive progress and growth in AI, Alibaba still trades at multiples that are unheard-of for AI companies in any country not called China. At today’s prices, it trades at 13 times adjusted earnings, 16 times reported earnings, a 1.69 PEG ratio, two times sales, and 1.8 times book.

Compared to non-Chinese tech companies, it’s a steal–at least going by multiples and nothing else.

We do need to look at more than just multiples to value a stock properly. Typically, investors look at discounted cash flows as well as multiples. In Alibaba’s case, DCF modelling is made complicated by the fact that the company is currently undergoing massive capital expenditures. However, if we take TTM EPS in place of free cash flow, assume that the company can grow at its 10 year CAGR earnings growth rate (18%) for five years before ceasing to grow, then with a 10% discount rate, it’s worth $154.90. These assumptions are fairly conservative; if the company’s cloud and international commerce segments keep up their growth then we should achieve the scenario just outlined.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on Alibaba’s June quarter earnings release is that it was much better than it looked at first glance. Though it missed on the headline metrics due to some technicalities, the release showed incredible strength under the hood. Alibaba stock rallied 9% after the release came out and honestly I’m not surprised. The company looks destined to become Asia's AI data center leader.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IAI: Exceptional Returns Make It An Attractive Alternative To High Beta Tech And Growth ETFs
data.description:
While the market saw stunning performances from tech ETFs in the past five years, the iShares U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchanges ETF (NYSEARCA:IAI) seems to be an exceptional case. IAI has been beating the S&P 500 by a whopping amount as its portfolio holdings are growing at a robust pace with the strong future fundamentals. In addition, the broker-dealers & securities exchanges focused-fund outclassed the fast-growing tech category.

Therefore, I initiate coverage of IAI with a strong buy rating.

U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchanges are Shining

Over the past few years, the financials sector has been demonstrating strong resilience against the tight credit market conditions with a constant risk of recession. Despite that, the sector continues to deliver record-breaking profits.

In the second quarter, the financials sector generated 13% year over year earnings growth, significantly topping Wall Street expectations of only a 2% increase.

The capital markets industry, which includes broker-dealers and securities exchanges, reported 22% year over year earnings growth in the second quarter.

The industry continued to witness a positive impact of increasing trading volume, particularly from retail investors. Investors chasing famous tech stocks and finding opportunities in the AI market is contributing to trading volume.

In the case of broker-dealers, the digitalization is playing a key role in onboarding millions of new investors.

For example, Robinhood Markets (HOOD), the online trading and investing app, saw stunning growth in the past few years, fueling exceptional share price gains for shareholders. Robinhood's share price rallied nearly 400% in the last twelve months alone.

In the second quarter, its earnings per share of $0.42 surpassed Wall Street expectation of $0.31 per share. The year over year earnings per share growth was 100%. The second quarter revenue of $989 million increased 45% compared to the year-ago quarter. Here is what Jason Warnick, chief financial officer, said:

Q3 is off to a great start in July, as customers accelerated their net deposits to around $6 billion and leaned in with strong trading across categories."

On the other hand, the asset managers and security exchange companies also benefit from the robust increase in trading volume and equity gains.

For example, Goldman Sachs (GS), the global banking, asset & wealth management, and platform solutions provider, saw a staggering 49% share price rally in the last twelve months.

The company's stock price uptrend is attributed to its robust financial numbers.

In the second quarter, Goldman Sachs earnings per share of $10.91 exceeded the estimate of $9.63. Its revenue of $14.6 billion increased 14% from the year ago period.

Whereas, the President of Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), Benjamin R. Jackson, attributes the company's strongest results in its history to the record volumes.

In the first half, the exchange traded over 1 billion contracts, including an all-time high of 673 million energy and 462 million interest rate contracts.

Moreover, CME Group's (CME) benchmark futures and options demand soared to a record level in the second quarter. In the June quarter, its year over year average daily volume increase of 16% helped it achieve record financial numbers.

Data providers, including S&P Global (SPGI), are also shining. The company reported 8% revenue and 10% year over year earnings growth in the June quarter. The company also increased its full year earnings per share outlook.

IAI Is A Solid Investment Vehicle

As the broker-dealers and securities exchanges are flourishing, it's crucial to select the right investment vehicle to capitalize on the uptrend.

Investing in a single stock, such as Robinhood or Goldman Sachs, may help investors generate lofty returns. However, the risk factor is also higher with a single stock investment. In addition, a single stock investment may not help investors capitalize on the broader industry trends.

Therefore, investment vehicles, such as iShares U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchanges ETF, are worth considering. The passively managed ETF enables investors to track the performance of 38 broker-dealers & securities exchanges through a single investment.

IAI's portfolio is concentrated heavily in its top 10 holdings, accounting for 72% of the overall portfolio. These are the largest companies in their respective markets.

IAI's top 10 holdings include Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley (MS), S&P Global, Robinhood, Coinbase (COIN), Moody's (MCO), MSCI Inc (MSCI) and a few others.

As discussed above, the majority of IAI's top stock holdings are delivering exceptional financial numbers and share price returns.

Consequently, IAI's share price rallied 35% in the last twelve months, beating the S&P 500 gain of 15% by more than 120%.

The fund's share price is also up much more year to date compared to the broader market index. In the last five years, shares of IAI increased 168%. Its total returns were around 191%. The fund offers a dividend yield of nearly 1%.

IAI's performance appears exceptional, given its outperformance compared to the S&P 500 index.

In fact, its returns are also significantly greater than the fast-growing tech market.

For example, the tech-focused and growth funds, such as the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK), Vanguard Mega Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (MGK), Vanguard Information Technology Index Fund ETF (VGT) and iShares US Technology ETF (IYW), underperformed compared to IAI in the last twelve months and three years.

IAI Characteristics and Quant Rating

iShares U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchanges ETF appears to be a solid investment vehicle for short- to long term investors.

IAI's liquidity is strong with $147 billion in assets under management and 131K in average shares trading volume. A healthy liquidity indicates investor interest. It also creates ease in the trading process. The fund's expense ratio of 0.38% is down from the median of all ETFs of 0.50%.

Moreover, despite a solid share price upside, IAI is trading in-line with the S&P 500 index. Its trailing price to earnings ratio hovers around 27x while price to book ratio is 3.83.

IAI also earned a buy rating with a high score of 4.23, according to the Seeking Alpha quant analysis. The quant rating is crucial because analyzing investment vehicles based on the data-driven approach helps in eliminating emotions.

Risk Factors to Consider

iShares U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchanges ETF is exposed to multiple risk factors. Technically, IAI's standard deviation of 24 and annualized volatility of 25% is higher than the median of all ETFs.

In addition, the fund's concentration in the top 10 holdings increases the risk factor. IAI may also respond to the broader market indices. Its high beta of 1.14 indicates that it can fall at a higher rate than the S&P 500 index during downtrends.

In Conclusion

In my opinion, iShares U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchanges ETF is one of the best investment vehicles to capitalize on the flourishing broker-dealers and securities exchanges industry.

IAI has been substantially beating the S&P 500 and fast-growing tech ETFs, making it an attractive option for investors seeking to earn lofty returns. The fund's dividend factor is adding to its strong returns. In addition, IAI's valuations are in-line with the S&P 500, while the expense ratio and liquidity factors are greater than the median of all ETFs.

data.title: Why I'm Buying International Paper Despite Its Premium Valuation
data.description:
Thesis

My research suggests International Paper Company (NYSE: NYSE:IP) is at a turning point. This century-old manufacturer has moved toward markets with better price power and stable demand by carefully choosing which businesses to buy and sell and leaving behind those that don't bring in enough money. Based on today’s price, the stock does seem slightly expensive. But the combination of a strategic reset, a stronger growth profile, and a dependable dividend supports the case for rapid multiple normalization.

A New Center of Gravity for International Paper

I saw International Paper cross a line at the beginning of the year that it can't go back from. When the company bought DS Smith in early 2025, it did more than just buy customers and plants. It also changed the company's center of gravity. The reason why IP sold PALM Group five mills—three in Normandy, one in Portugal, and one in Spain—was to get approval from Brussels. The assets weren't very important, so management didn't waste political capital protecting them.

At the end of the most recent quarter, after the deal, IP had 65,000 employees in 30 countries, with its main offices in Memphis and London. I see that it also gave the company a second passport: its shares now trade in the UK under the name IPC, which is a clear sign of its plans to expand across the Atlantic.

The purchase dropped IP into a market that was already becoming more consolidated. Capacity was being taken away, demand trends were changing, and this was driven by early 2025 linerboard price hikes by North American makers that showed what they meant to do. Smurfit WestRock (SW) shut down mills in the U.S., Georgia-Pacific (a private Koch Industries company, btw) shut down Cedar Springs, and then IP permanently shut down its Georgia sites while putting more money into its Selma, Alabama site.

Nothing about this was chance. The sector was taking out unnecessary tons on purpose, increasing usage, and restoring price control that had been lost during the last cycle. Here, obviously, higher rates did the heavy lifting. The board began over. Those who moved first were in a good position to run the new balance.

By August, the DS Smith deal was over, and IP made its next big move: it agreed to sell American Industrial Partners its Global Cellulose Fibers business. In 2024, GCF made $2.5 billion and was the market leader in fluff pulp and specialty fabrics. However, it no longer fit the story IP wished to tell. Pulp needs a lot of cash and is unpredictable. Management thinks that packaging gives them more stable price power and the chance to build long-term ties with customers.

The sale is set to go through in the fourth quarter of 2025. After the transaction concludes, IP will only be a packaging company. This will make it easier to run, easier for investors to value, and less affected by the early-year increases in pulp prices. Markets took notice. Bank of America Securities raised its rating on IP to "Buy," citing both valuations and signs that pricing power was growing faster than expected and very specific thesis that containerboard prices could rise by $60 per ton by 2026, with operating rates surpassing 95%.

International Paper Balances Gains and Growing Pains

Revenue reached $6.77 billion at the end of the last quarter, up 43% from the same time last year and about $65 million more than expected. Folding in DS Smith's operations was a big reason for this, but it wasn't the only one. Price and mix added $0.21 per share in North America. This shows that earlier price changes are sticking and that big customers are responding to better service. Management linked this to a bigger goal: to make $6 billion in EBITDA by 2027. Yes, that's an ambitious objective, but I think reasoning behind it is pretty simple: use size, price, and efficiency to raise earnings to a level that the market hasn't previously recognized IP for.

Unfortunately, the quarter reminded us how difficult changes can be. Adjusted EPS was $0.20, which was $0.21 less than what analysts were expecting. For context, operations and costs were $119 million worse than the previous quarter. One-time events like revaluing inventory and natural gas curtailments at a North American mill caused some of the more larger losses, totaling $18 million.

Management’s take on it was that these were one-off pressures, so not exactly a new norm, or a reset of the baseline. Fair enough. But they also showed how many pieces still have to click together. Take energy prices, especially in Europe, which are jumpy. Specifically, with Norwegian supply jitters pushing TTF higher at times.

Now, having said that, focusing only on the misses would miss the real progress on operations. In North America, IP cut its volume gap to the industry by 200 basis points in Q2 and expects to close it completely by year-end.

Also worth noting, delivery operations have gotten better, with on-time shipments going from 92% around the end of last year, to 97%. And, the most important source where the money comes from, the clients, seem to be happy about it. According to management, they're indicating that the major national account seem to be buttering up to these improvements, and have started to give IP more share.

Obviously, none of what's happening can be chalked up to luck. Credit's to be given to IP's 80/20, used in 40 plants with the goal of blending that into 75 of them before the new year takes over. The backdrop helped too, thanks to U.S. e-commerce returning to year-over-year growth, with parcel traffic picking up, and July’s promotions driving heavy online spending.

Headwinds Abroad, Progress at Home

Moving on, we see that cost transformation was a nice, bright spot for the company. First-half actions put IP at a $650 million run rate, that's already past the $600 million goal for the year, and on track for $1.9 billion in savings by 2027. The Lighthouse plant model, now scaling across the network, is starting to deliver the same productivity gains seen in early adopters. So based on the data currently available, I think it's fair to assume that Q2 should mark the bottom on this front.

For instance, there were scheduled outages, and those racked up bills around $39 million in North America and $37 million in GCF. But, with fewer ahead, management sees earnings improving in Q3. And the timing seems to be lining up with broader industry shifts with portfolio pruning and mill conversions that are resetting cost structures. IP’s own conversions and closures this month fit that playbook. The bet is that a smaller, better invested mill base can run harder, hold pricing, and avoid the reliability problems of the past. Although, Europe's soft underbelly's giving a very slight drag. to the momentum. Nothing too major though. EMEA box shipments fell about 1% from the prior quarter, dragged down by April and May before a modest rebound in June and July.

Energy in the region stayed volatile, and new rules are adding pressure. The EU’s Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation, set for 2025, raises the bar on recyclability and reporting. In the UK, Extended Producer Responsibility fees kick in this October. Having said that, none of this threatens the business outright, but it does add cost and complexity that management will have to stay on top of.

Looking at the picture today, operations and costs ran $17 million worse, and while DS Smith’s European assets added $64 million of adjusted EBITDA, the contribution fell short of early expectations. Therefore, management has rightfully shifted Europe to the lower end of its $900 million to $1.1 billion EBITDA target, pointing to the usual suspects of market volatility and, yes, tariff uncertainty.

GCF, still working through its own transition, added to the drag. Outages weighed on earnings, with operations and costs $18 million worse than last quarter, even as price and mix contributed $30 million. Another hit is coming in Q3 as energy credits roll off, cutting about $36 million.

International Paper’s Synergies Must Now Deliver

On an absolute basis, the balance sheet took its toll this quarter. Corporate items were $0.16 per share unfavorable, driven by higher interest from DS Smith debt and a larger share count after the merger. Depreciation offered a small offset, but the added assets also bring recurring amortization. I would say none of this was unexpected, but it does lift the bar for synergies to show through. The London listing, an unavoidable byproduct of the deal, gives IP a bigger stage and a wider audience, I'd guess along with sharper scrutiny too.

In this case, management didn't sugarcoat the truth about mill efficiency. The issue goes back a long way. Because of years of underinvestment and repeated breakdowns, the company has lost about $150 million in profit so far this year, or $300 million if you add it up over a year. The plan is simple: put money back into key mills and get out of the ones that aren't doing well. In any case, the difference is clear. The businesses that convert things went through their own two-year makeover and are now adding to the results.

Now the job is to follow the same rules in the mills. Based on the information we have right now, I think the reward could be substantial, but it will take time and money. For example, the way the industry works makes the case stronger: competitors are reducing their capacity, like Smurfit WestRock and Georgia-Pacific by closing Cedar Springs. This means that more work is going to the mills that are still open. For IP, this makes dependability upgrades even more valuable because they allow for higher usage and the pricing premium that comes with it.

Unfortunately, the state of the economy as a whole put even more pressure on things. Single-family activity is still slow in the U.S., permits and building confidence are falling, and new home sales fell again in July. Spending on construction has slowed down, which has reduced the market for building goods, which are packaged in boxes. Tariffs and wider global tensions also slow down industrial production and change how businesses handle their stock.

The ISM "customers' inventories" gauge is below 50, which is usually a sign that businesses should restock, but they have been slow to do so in 2025. And this is what the government had to say: inventories stayed the same or went down in late spring and early summer. The way IP frames the split is unambiguous: the U.S. business is growing and becoming easier to manage, while Europe is still affected by market and policy changes in other countries. Essentially, that gap doesn't look like it's going to be closing any time soon.

International Paper’s Valuation

It's all about valuation in the end. IP trades at $48.96 (the time of this analysis) on a blended P/E of 32.7x, which I think is way too high for a packaging business. In the past, its multiple has been around 15–17x, and the norm for the sector is much lower than the current number.

In this case, the non-GAAP P/E is 27.1x, which is almost 60% higher than the sector norm of 16.9x and almost 60% higher than IP's own five-year average. A forward P/E of 35x is more than 80% above the market and double the company's five-year average, which isn't a great sign.

Still, things look different if you believe the profit path that management and analysts are predicting. EPS is expected to go up by 57% in 2025, 74% in 2026, and 28% 2027. If that happens, the forward P/E will drop to 12.3x by 2027, which is less of a premium and more of a deal for a world leader with better economies.

On the call, CEO Silvernail said something that seems to sum this up: "It's not lost on anybody that this is a big inflection point for us." Therefore, if the overall transformation all works out (pricing sticks, outages ease, and costs come out) valuation could reset quickly.

And if you compare today's stock price to estimates of its fair value, which is around $79, I think it's a good deal. Some long-term models, with a very strong emphasis on hypothetical, mind you, even predict an upside of more than $200 over the cycle.

Yes, management is achieving nice growth, and the growth metrics make the case more believable. Revenue climbed 18.1% year over year, far ahead of the sector’s 2.2%, and is projected to grow 13.5% going forward, about 4x the industry pace. EBITDA was up 22.8% and is expected to expand another 25%. EBIT is forecast to rise more than 26%. GAAP EPS jumped 91% last year, with another 52% growth projected.

In the long run, over the next three to five years, EPS is expected to grow at a CAGR of 36.9%, which is 3x the 12% growth rate seen in the field. It's interesting to compare this to IP's own five-year past, during which both sales and EBITDA were flat to negative.

Last but not least, the payout provides some stability. IP pays 3.8%, which is almost twice as much as the norm in its sector. Growth here isn't exactly very high, about 1% forward, but the payment at least keeps income buyers somewhat anchored during a volatile transition.

Final Takeaway

I'm calling IP a Buy here. Based on current results, the stock price is technically expensive. However, if management follows the growth path that experts expect, the multiple then quickly drops to a much more appealing level. With sales and EBITDA growing much faster than the industry as a whole and a dividend return almost twice that of peers, I think the company's move to become a pure-play packaging leader has enough growth potential to support a positive outlook, even though there are risks in the short term.

data.title: G Mining: Robust Self-Financing Strategy In Brazil And Second Mine Building Decision Approaching
data.description:
A Buy Rating for Shares of G Mining Ventures

This article recommends a Buy rating for US-listed shares of G Mining Ventures Corp. (OTCPK:GMINF). This rating is consistent with our previous article.

Hereinafter, G Mining Ventures Corp. may be referred to as “G Mining”, “GMINF”, or “GMIN”.

G Mining Ventures Corp. receives another buy recommendation. Rising gold prices, robust gold production at the Brazilian Tocantinzinho mine on the back of the expansion to the desired capacity of the mill facility, and controlled costs confirm the solid foundation of the company's self-funded growth strategy. The company expects a construction decision for its long-term, low-cost, and profitable Oko West gold mining project in Guyana, which would become its second gold-producing mine. In addition, the Brazilian Federal Court in Maranhão has approved the resumption of the permitting process for drilling activities at Gurupi, its second mineral deposit in Brazil.

Who is G Mining Ventures Corp., Headquartered in Brossard, Quebec, Canada

In its report on financial and operating results for the three and six months ended June 30, 2025, G Mining Ventures Corp. describes itself in the section “About G Mining Ventures Corp.” as follows:

G Mining Ventures Corp. is a mining company engaged in the acquisition, exploration and development of precious metal projects to capitalize on the value uplift from successful mine development. GMIN is well-positioned to grow into the next mid-tier precious metals producer by leveraging strong access to capital and proven development expertise. GMIN is currently anchored by the Tocantinzinho Mine in Brazil, supported by the Gurupi Project in Brazil and the Oko West Project in Guyana — all with significant exploration upside and located in mining-friendly jurisdictions. GMIN trades on the TSX under the symbol "GMIN".

Since Our Last Buy Rating for G Mining Ventures Corp.: How The Stock Has Developed

As expected, the company was able to build on its strength thanks to the gold hype and increased production in Tocantinzinho, Brazil. This raises the chances of expanded financing options for Guyana's next high-margin gold mine, the Oko West project. The stock market will hardly remain unaffected by these developments. It just takes time. As we will explain later in this analysis, there are some interesting upside catalysts that potential buyers should not miss. Since our last analysis on June 4, 2025, GMIN shares are now 5.51% lower in market value, lagging behind the S&P 500's change of +8.83%. But now G Mining shares are no longer bearish, but in a recovery phase after a trend reversal shortly after the end of July 2025. However, as we have suggested, had investors added their positions when the share prices were significantly lower than now, around the end of July, their investments would have performed much better overall, possibly even better than the entire US stock market.

G Mining Ventures Corp. Asset Portfolio

The Tocantinzinho Mine (“TZ”), located 202 km south of Itaituba, Brazil, is an operating open-pit gold mine wholly owned by G Mining. The renewable energy-powered Tocantinzinho Gold Mine began commercial production in September 2024 and reached its planned capacity in the second quarter of 2025.

For Louis-Pierre Gignac, President and CEO, this development marked a decisive “step-change in gold production and free cash flow,” prompting him to describe the second quarter as “a pivotal quarter for GMIN.” (source: G Mining Ventures reports strong Q2 2025 results)

More specifically, between May and June 2025, the Tocantinzinho Processing Plant reached its design capacity, operating at 96% of its planned throughput of 12,890 tonnes per day.

The Tocantinzinho mine increased its production by 20% to 42,587 ounces in the second quarter of 2025, compared to the previous quarter and produced 78,165 ounces in the first half of 2025, or

“45% of the lower end of our annual guidance range.”, the company said in its Q2 and H1 2025 report, adding: “GMIN reaffirms its full-year 2025 production guidance of 175,000 to 200,000 ounces of gold with an expectation of being at the lower end of guidance.”

The Tocantinzinho mine had estimated proven and probable mineral reserves of 51,057,000 tonnes grading 1.24 g/t Au (grams of gold per tonne of mineral), containing 2,031,000 ounces of gold, as of December 31, 2024, along with an additional 4,710,000 tonnes of measured and indicated mineral resources grading 1.22 g/t Au, containing 150,000 ounces of gold, and an additional 752,000 tonnes of inferred resources grading 1.12 g/t Au, containing 27,000 ounces, as of December 31, 2024. Mineral resources are not mineral reserves, and their economic viability is not proven. Based on the mineral reserve estimate and assumed annual gold production of 175,000 to 200,000 ounces of gold, the Tocantinzinho mine and processing plant complex has an operating life of 10.2 to 11.6 years, with the possibility of an extension if mineral resources are explored and developed into reserves.

Due to the increase in production, which benefited from the crucial second quarter in which GMIN achieved nominal throughput at TZ, and higher gold prices on higher gold ounce sales volumes, while costs, although also higher, were fully within management's control due to expected one-off higher cost factors, GMIN achieved the following improvement in financial results:

Quarter-over-quarter:

  • Revenue increased 32.2% to $129.6 million.
  • Adjusted net income, at $0.16 per share, increased 3.1% to $36.5 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA increased 35.4% to $92.9 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin of 71.6% of revenue increased 170 basis points from 69.9%.
  • Cash flow from operating activities increased 161.3% to $79.8 million.
  • Free cash flow increased 67.5% to $60.2 million.

GMIN has more available liquidity at the end of Q2 2025, as illustrated in the following screenshot:

The key positive factors of the improved funds, such as the gold price, production, and cost control, which are on track to continue improving, were so strong that GMIN did not need to rely on additional borrowing capital. Total debt even decreased from $114.8 million on December 30, 2024, to $108.5 million on June 30, 2025. GMIN could fully financially support the further development of its 100% interest in Oko West in Guyana and its 100% interest in Gurupi in Brazil, and even increase its liquidity by $14.904 million from $141.215 million on December 30, 2024, to $156.119 million on June 30, 2025.

The Outlook: Higher Gold Production, Lower Costs and Rising Gold Prices

GMIN expects robust gold production in the second half of 2025, supported by higher-grade ore and the ramp-up of production at TZ, as well as flexibility in mill feed management. The costs are expected to decline in the second half of 2025. In its report of operating and financial results for the second quarter of 2025 and the first half of 2025, GMIN states the following:

Cash costs (2) were $763 per ounce, while AISC (2) totaled $1,355 per ounce, largely driven by the timing of sustaining capital expenditures, including non-recurring mobile fleet purchases. As these one-time investments are now complete, the Corporation anticipates AISC will decline in the second half of the year.

The company had previously issued an AISC forecast that was adjusted to $1,025 to $1,155 per ounce sold.

The price of gold: An upward trend is expected in the coming years. We consider the following gold price forecasts from analysts, most recently published. In this regard, Yahoo Finance writes:

Goldman Sachs sees an even more bullish scenario for the precious metal, noting in a deep dive earlier this year that a less independent Fed "could dent the appeal of the Dollar and USTs, but may make gold shine even brighter."

Last week, analysts reaffirmed their $4,000 per troy ounce forecast for mid-2026, "driven by structurally strong central bank demand and ETF-inflows" supported by Fed easing.

Development Projects Can Gain Momentum

G Mining Ventures Corp. can now give even more momentum to its development and growth projects, which will also help improve market sentiment around the US-listed GMIN stock. And if the share price confirms this, shareholders should get value for their money.

In April 2025, a positive feasibility study was completed for the Oko West project, located 114 km southwest of Georgetown, Guyana. It confirmed the potential for long-term, low-cost, and profitable gold mining. According to GMIN's Q2 2025 and H1 2025 Operations and Financial Report, the key highlights of the Oko West project are (the company writes):

Average annual production of 350,000 ounces over a 12.3-year mine life AISC of $1,123 per ounce and initial capital cost of $972 million Base case after-tax NPV 5% of $2.2 billion and IRR of 27% at a gold price of $2,500 per ounce Upside case after-tax NPV 5% of $3.2 billion and IRR of 35% at a gold price of $3,000 per ounce

Oko West is a financially sound project. This aspect was already discussed in the previous article. However, we would like to reiterate that the Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) is currently at US$3,416.36/oz (at the time of this writing) – an increase of 30.17% year-to-date and 36.21% over the past 12 months. According to Goldman Sachs' optimistic scenario, it will rise to a higher level of US$4,000/oz by mid-2026. We can approximate the Net Present Value (NPV5%) that would be with different gold price assumptions than in the feasibility study by assuming a linear relationship between the gold price and the NPV5% based on the inputs we know, and assuming that the mathematical equation is NPV5% = 2 x Gold Price - 2800. Now we replace the equation's independent variable “gold price” with the current gold price of $3,416.36/oz and the gold price forecast of $4,000/oz. We get an NPV5% of $4,032.72 million and $5,200 million, respectively. Given that the project could become significantly more valuable if gold prices climb, market sentiment is subject to forces that could make it feel even more positive than it does today, with potentially upside consequences for GMIN's share price.

Following the granting of preliminary environmental approval in January 2025, work is currently underway to complete key infrastructure facilities. These, along with engineering work at Oko West, will continue in 2025.

All circumstances support G Mining Ventures Corp.'s self-funded growth strategy and raise the likelihood that the company will secure project financing for Oko West and make a construction decision before the end of 2025. In our view, the construction decision could provide a remarkable boost to the sentiment surrounding the share price.

Gurupi, 46 km southwest of Maracaçumé, Brazil, has received a favourable ruling from the Brazilian Federal Court of Maranhão, confirming GMIN's right to resume the permitting process for the drilling activities.

In its second quarter and first half of 2025 operating and financial report, G Mining Ventures Corp. states:

The Gurupi Project, located in Maranhão State, Brazil, hosts 1.83 million ounces of indicated and 0.77 million ounces of inferred mineral resources across a large and prospective land package. The Corporation is reviewing historical exploration data and assessing future drilling opportunities to evaluate potential resource expansion and further validate the geological model. In light of recent positive developments, an additional $4 million budget has been allocated to launch an inaugural drill program in the second half of the year.

The Share Price Is Recovering. Buy When The Next Dip Comes

The US-listed shares of G Mining Ventures Corp. under the symbol “GMINF” were trading at $14.79/share, giving it a market capitalisation of $3.38 billion at the time of this writing. The shares are in a recovery phase, and are above the MA Ribbon and closer to the upper limit than the lower limit of the 52-week range of $5.83/share to $16.27/share.

The RSI of 64.41 indicates that there is still room for growth, and investors should reinforce their investments in this promising gold company as soon as they have the opportunity. We think they should do so, continuing to take advantage of dips when they occur, as this strategy gives investors a better chance of their investment performing in a satisfactory manner, even when the share prices have temporarily taken a downward path.

The decline that occurred between mid- and late July should not worry investors, as GMIN did not experience any operational or financial setbacks; on the contrary, as we have illustrated, they are progressing very well. The decline reflects a shift by Chinese investors from gold to equities amid record ETF outflows. GMIN is suffering more from the impact of stagnant gold prices than benefiting from rising stock prices. The share price is linked to the US stock market with a 24M beta of 0.67 (see Seeking Alpha here under the “Risk” section on the “GMINF G Mining Ventures Corp.” page), meaning the price will rise much more slowly when the overall US stock market rises. But since GMIN's share price fell as the US-listed equities rose, it means that the share price is linked to changes in the gold price through a very robust gold beta. This creates the opportunity for a decline that potential buyers can exploit if interest rates remain higher for longer than expected. Gold is not a yield-generating instrument and suffers in a high-interest-rate environment. The US Federal Reserve is likely to cut interest rates by 25 basis points to a range of 400-425 basis points at its meeting on September 17. FedWatch currently estimates the probability of this scenario at 85.1%. In the subsequent period, the tariff war will fuel a highly uncertain future, with concerns about a resurgence of inflationary risk. In response, US central bankers could decide to resume a wait-and-see approach after the September rate cut. Prices of zero-yielding gold bullion will then decline or stagnate, and gold's robust beta could lead to another visible low in GMIN stock price, similar to that seen in mid- to late-July 2025.

Conclusion

G Mining Ventures Corp. is a mining company that also explores and develops gold mining projects. Its flagship project is the Tocantinzinho gold project in the Brazilian state of Pará. Having reached its optimal mill capacity, the company is experiencing robust gold production as the gold price continues to rise. This project generates solid cash flow and enables the further development of the Oko West gold mining project in Guyana. This project could become G Mining's second large, high-margin gold mining project within a few years. The company could receive project financing and make a construction decision by the end of 2025. The share price is rising, and investors may want to add shares to their positions once the share price dips on the US stock market.

By buying shares during price dips, the investor can strengthen the position and make it more resilient to potential negative consequences resulting from this stock's low liquidity. Seeking Alpha shows that in the “Trading Data” section, 76,361 shares changed hands daily, which corresponds to the “average volume (3 months).”
